title: 2 Corinthians 2
subtitle: Forgiveness and the Triumph of the Gospel

heb.paragraph: The noun lypē (grief, sorrow) and its cognate verb lypeō (to grieve) appear seven times in vv. 1–5, creating a dense network of grief-language. Paul’s grief is not self-pity but relational anguish: his joy depends on the Corinthians’ wellbeing (v. 2). The ‘tearful letter’ (v. 4) was written ‘out of great distress and anguish of heart,’ not to inflict pain but to communicate the depth of his love.

refs.note: Paul returns to the tearful letter in ch. 7, reflecting on the godly sorrow it produced. The two passages bracket the theological argument of chs. 3–6.
Some scholars identify the offender of 2 Cor 2 with the incestuous man of 1 Cor 5, though this identification is disputed.

notes.note: Paul’s decision not to make ‘another painful visit’ reveals the depth of the relational crisis. The ‘painful visit’ (second visit) was apparently a disaster: Paul was publicly challenged, and the church failed to support him.

The tearful letter was an act of courageous love, not punitive anger. Paul wrote ‘not to grieve you but to let you know the depth of my love’ (v. 4). Discipline motivated by love, not resentment, is the mark of genuine pastoral authority.

Calvin reads the tearful letter as a model of pastoral discipline: correction must proceed from love, not malice. The pastor who rebukes without grief has failed to understand his calling. Calvin notes that Paul’s tears (v. 4) authenticate the rebuke: severity from a grieving heart is received very differently from severity from an indifferent one.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note discusses the chronological puzzle: ‘another painful visit’ implies a previous painful visit (the second visit), making the planned visit the third (cf. 12:14; 13:1). The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, Pauline Christology (2007) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee argues that the tearful letter demonstrates the paradox of apostolic authority: Paul’s power is exercised through vulnerability, not domination. His tears are not weakness but the authentic expression of a love that cannot bear estrangement from his children in the faith.

harris.source: Murray J. Harris, 2 Corinthians, NIGTC/EGC (2005) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Harris provides detailed reconstruction of the events between 1 and 2 Corinthians, arguing that the painful visit and tearful letter represent a distinct crisis from the issues addressed in 1 Corinthians. The offender (v. 5) was likely someone who publicly insulted Paul during the second visit, not the incestuous man of 1 Cor 5.

hist.context: Paul refers to a previous ‘painful visit’ (v. 1) and a subsequent ‘tearful letter’ (vv. 3–4), neither of which can be identified with 1 Corinthians. The painful visit was apparently a confrontation in which Paul was publicly humiliated by an individual (the offender of vv. 5–11). The tearful letter, now lost, demanded that the church discipline this person. The sequence of events is: (1) 1 Corinthians; (2) painful visit; (3) tearful letter; (4) Titus’s mission; (5) 2 Corinthians.

heb.paragraph: The verb charizomai (to forgive, to show grace) appears three times in vv. 7–10. The term is cognate with charis (grace): forgiveness is a concrete act of grace-giving. Paul’s forgiveness is ‘in the sight of Christ’ (en prosōpō Christou, v. 10)—Christ is the witness and guarantor of the restoration. The community’s forgiveness ratifies and participates in Christ’s own gracious disposition.

refs.note: Jesus’ parable of the unmerciful servant establishes the principle that those who have received forgiveness must extend it. The parallel with Paul’s instruction is close: forgiveness is not optional but mandatory for the forgiven community.
Paul’s warning not to ‘give the devil a foothold’ parallels the spiritual-warfare reasoning of 2 Cor 2:11: unresolved anger and unforgiveness create openings for satanic exploitation.

notes.note: The church has disciplined the offender, and the discipline has worked: the man has repented. Paul now commands restoration: ‘forgive and comfort him’ (v. 7). The goal of church discipline is always restoration, not permanent exclusion.

Paul reveals a secondary purpose for his letter: to test the Corinthians’ obedience (v. 9). His own forgiveness is given ‘in the sight of Christ’ (v. 10)—a phrase that invests the act with christological gravity. The warning about Satan’s schemes (v. 11) reveals that unforgiveness is not merely a pastoral issue but a battlefield in the spiritual war.

Calvin extracts the principles of church discipline: (1) discipline must be proportionate (‘the punishment inflicted by the majority is sufficient’); (2) its goal is restoration, not punishment; (3) extended exclusion risks driving the offender to despair; (4) failure to forgive exposes the community to satanic exploitation. Calvin warns against both excessive leniency (which encourages sin) and excessive severity (which destroys the sinner).

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note discusses ‘the punishment inflicted by the majority’ (hē epitimia hautē hē hypo tōn pleionōn): the term epitimia (punishment, censure) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, suggesting a formal congregational decision rather than spontaneous ostracism.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, Pauline Christology (2007) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee reads the forgiveness command as evidence that Paul’s authority functions within a congregational framework: the church disciplined the offender, and Paul now guides the restoration. Fee emphasises the corporate dimension: the whole community participates in both discipline and forgiveness. The satanic-strategy warning (v. 11) reflects Paul’s conviction that the community’s relational health has cosmic implications.

harris.source: Murray J. Harris, 2 Corinthians, NIGTC/EGC (2005) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Harris provides the most detailed analysis of the identity of the offender, arguing against the traditional identification with the incestuous man of 1 Cor 5. The offence was more likely a personal affront to Paul during the painful visit. Harris notes that Paul’s language shifts from third person (‘he has grieved all of you’) to first person (‘what I have forgiven’), underscoring that the primary victim of the offence was Paul himself, yet he refuses to hold grudges.

hist.context: The offender has been disciplined by ‘the majority’ (v. 6)—indicating a congregational decision—and Paul now urges restoration. The pastoral logic is precise: the punishment has achieved its purpose (repentance); continued ostracism would be destructive, causing the offender to be ‘overwhelmed by excessive sorrow’ (v. 7). Paul introduces a spiritual-warfare dimension: failure to forgive gives Satan an opportunity (v. 11). Unforgiveness is not merely a relational failure but a strategic vulnerability.

heb.paragraph: The verb thriambeuō (to lead in triumphal procession) in v. 14 draws on the Roman triumph (triumphus)—the ceremonial parade in which a victorious general displayed his captives and spoils. The image is complex: Paul is not the triumphant general but the captive in God’s procession. Yet this is not humiliation but honour: to be led as God’s captive is to participate in God’s victory. The incense metaphor extends the imagery: in a Roman triumph, incense was burned along the route. Paul is the ‘aroma of Christ’—a fragrance of life to the saved and death to the perishing.

The verb kapēleuō (to peddle, to huckster) in v. 17 denotes the practice of adulterating goods for profit—specifically, the watering down of wine by unscrupulous merchants. Paul contrasts himself with those who ‘peddle the word of God for profit.’ The accusation is directed at the super-apostles who exploit the gospel for financial gain. Paul speaks ‘with sincerity, as those sent from God.’

refs.note: The triumph metaphor recurs in Colossians, where Christ ‘made a public spectacle’ of the powers and authorities, ‘triumphing over them by the cross.’ In both passages, the triumph is God’s, not Paul’s.
Christ as a ‘fragrant offering and sacrifice to God’ parallels the aroma-of-Christ imagery: Paul’s ministry emits the fragrance of Christ’s self-offering.

notes.note: Paul’s arrival in Troas found an ‘open door’ for the gospel but no Titus. His anxiety for news from Corinth was so intense that he abandoned a fruitful ministry and pressed on to Macedonia. This remarkable detail reveals Paul’s pastoral priorities: the wellbeing of the Corinthian church mattered more than evangelistic opportunity.

The triumphal-procession metaphor transforms Paul’s apparent weakness (anxiety, restless travel, uncertain outcomes) into participation in God’s cosmic victory parade. The aroma metaphor introduces a double effect: the same gospel is life to believers and death to those who reject it. Paul is not responsible for the different responses; he is simply the fragrance-bearer. The chapter closes with a devastating contrast: Paul speaks with sincerity, unlike those who ‘peddle’ God’s word.

Calvin reads the triumphal-procession image as a profound statement about the nature of gospel ministry: the preacher is not the general but the captive—led by God, not leading. Calvin emphasises the double effect of the aroma (v. 15–16): the same gospel that saves also condemns, depending on the hearer’s response. This places the responsibility on the audience, not the preacher. Calvin applies v. 17 to his own polemical context: ministers who adulterate the gospel for personal advantage are spiritual hucksters.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note discusses the debated meaning of thriambeuō: does Paul depict himself as a triumphant general or a captive in the procession? The NET favours the captive reading, noting that the standard usage of the verb means ‘to lead someone in a triumphal procession’ as a captive, not as the victor.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, Pauline Christology (2007) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee reads the triumphal-procession metaphor as the theological bridge between Paul’s personal narrative (vv. 12–13) and his extended defence of apostolic ministry (3:1–6:13). Paul’s weakness (anxiety, inability to rest) is not a contradiction of God’s triumph but its vehicle: God’s victory is displayed precisely through the frail, anxious, tear-stained apostle.

harris.source: Murray J. Harris, 2 Corinthians, NIGTC/EGC (2005) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Harris provides the most comprehensive analysis of the triumphal-procession imagery, drawing on Roman literary and archaeological sources. He argues that Paul deliberately positions himself as the captive—one who has been conquered by Christ and is now paraded as evidence of Christ’s victory. The aroma metaphor draws on both the Roman triumph (incense burned along the route) and the OT sacrificial system (the ‘pleasing aroma’ of sacrifice, Gen 8:21; Lev 1:9). Harris reads v. 17 as an early salvo in the polemic against the super-apostles: Paul’s ministry is characterised by sincerity (eilikrineia), theirs by mercenary exploitation (kapēleuō).

hist.context: Paul’s narrative suddenly shifts from personal travel (searching for Titus in Troas, v. 12–13) to a magnificent theological exclamation (v. 14). The abrupt transition suggests that Paul’s relief at eventually finding Titus (narrated in 7:5–7) was so overwhelming that he breaks into praise mid-sentence. The triumphal-procession metaphor inaugurates the great apologia of 2:14–7:4, in which Paul defends his apostolic ministry as an authentic expression of the new covenant.

rows.label: vv.1–4
rows.text: The tearful letter: love expressed through painful discipline

rows.label: vv.5–8
rows.text: Forgiveness commanded: restore the repentant offender

rows.label: vv.9–11
rows.text: Obedience test and satanic strategy behind unforgiveness

rows.label: vv.12–13
rows.text: Troas: open door abandoned for anxiety about Titus

rows.label: vv.14–17
rows.text: Triumph in Christ: captive, aroma, sincerity vs. hucksterism

lit.note: Chapter 2 transitions from relational crisis (tearful letter, forgiveness) to theological celebration (triumphal procession). The shift inaugurates the great apostolic apologia of 2:14–7:4.

themes.note: Mercy dominates as Paul commands forgiveness and celebrates God’s triumph through weakness. Mission scores highly as the aroma metaphor frames gospel ministry.

hebtext.note: The noun lypē (grief, sorrow) and its cognate verb lypeō (to grieve) appear seven times in vv. 1–5, creating a dense network of grief-language. Paul’s forgiveness is ‘in the sight of Christ’ (en prosōpō Christou, v. 10)—Christ is the witness and guarantor of the restoration. The verb kapēleuō (to peddle, to huckster) in v. 17 denotes the practice of adulterating goods for profit—specifically, the watering down of wine by unscrupulous merchants. Paul contrasts himself with those who ‘peddle the word of God for profit.’ The accusation is directed at the super-apostles who exploit the gospel for financial gain. Paul speaks ‘with sincerity, as those sent from God.’

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

trans.title: Comparing translations in Forgiveness and the Triumph of the Gospel

translations.text: For I wrote you out of great distress and anguish of heart and with many tears, not to grieve you but to let you know the depth of my love for you.
For I wrote to you out of much affliction and anguish of heart and with many tears, not to cause you pain but to let you know the abundant love that I have for you.
I wrote as I did, so that when I came I would not be distressed by those who should have made me rejoice. I had confidence in all of you, that you would all share my joy.
And I wrote as I did, so that when I came I might not suffer pain from those who should have made me rejoice, for I felt sure of all of you, that my joy would be the joy of you all.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Forgiveness and the Triumph of the Gospel within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

discourse.thesis: Paul's painful letter was written out of love — now it's time to forgive the offender.

nodes.text: Paul decided not to come in sorrow again. His painful letter was written through tears, not to grieve but to show his love.
The offender has been punished enough. Now forgive and comfort him, lest he be overwhelmed by excessive sorrow. Paul forgives for their sake.
Paul went to Troas for the gospel but found no rest because Titus wasn't there. He moved on to Macedonia.
Thanks be to God who leads us in triumphal procession! We are the aroma of Christ — life to some, death to others. We speak sincerely, not peddling God's word.

coaching.tip: 'I wrote to you out of much affliction and anguish of heart and with many tears' (v. 4). The 'severe letter' caused Paul pain to write. He's not a detached administrator but a wounded pastor. 'Not to cause you pain but to let you know the abundant love that I have for you' (v. 4). Confrontation costs the confronter.

'We are the aroma of Christ to God among those who are being saved and among those who are perishing' (v. 15). Same fragrance, opposite responses: life to life, death to death (v. 16). The gospel divides by its nature. Paul doesn't adjust the aroma to improve reception. Faithful ministry smells different to different people.

prayer_prompt: As Paul urges the church to forgive and restore the repentant offender before he is overwhelmed by excessive sorrow, consider asking God whether you have withheld restoration from someone whose repentance you should have accepted.
